key_insight: Atmospheric plasma-sprayed metal–graphite coatings can make titanium PEMFC bipolar plates both more corrosion-resistant and lower in contact resistance, with performance improving as graphite content increases.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical surface-engineering route to replace or protect metal bipolar plates in PEM fuel cells while preserving conductivity and durability.
method: APS deposited Ni–graphite and Cu–graphite coatings onto titanium bipolar plates
Varied graphite content to study phase composition, microstructure, corrosion resistance, and electrical conductivity
Evaluated performance using potentiostatic corrosion and interfacial contact resistance (ICR) measurements
evidence_signal: The coated plates achieved low potentiostatic corrosion current densities (1.05 × 10^-4 A·cm^-2 for Ni–G and 0.91 × 10^-4 A·cm^-2 for Cu–G) and improved ICR values, especially for Cu–G.
watch_item: Results are promising, but coating durability, adhesion, and long-term fuel-cell cycling performance still need validation before commercial deployment.
paper_title: Investigation of Metal-Graphite Coatings for PEMFC Bipolar Plate Using Atmospheric Plasma Thermal Spraying
